Then, it is straightforward to show that for every . i ; ai ; x / , P GLYPH&lt;3&gt; i . ai j x / D GLYPH&lt;3&gt;. ai j v P GLYPH&lt;3&gt; i . GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ; xt // . That is, P GLYPH&lt;3&gt; is a fixed point of P D GLYPH&lt;3&gt; .v P / , where GLYPH&lt;3&gt; .v P / GLYPH&lt;17&gt; f GLYPH&lt;3&gt;. ai j v P i . GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ; x // V for every . i ; ai ; x / g . Given the assumptions on the distribution of private information, we can define best response probability functions which are continuous in the compact set of player's choice probabilities. By Brower's theorem, there exists at least one equilibrium. In general, the equilibrium is not unique. 28

We now distinguish between observable and unobservable variables from the point of view of the econometrician. In principle, we could distinguish observable and unobservable components both in common knowledge and in private information state variables. We start with a more restrictive and simpler case.

ASSUMPTION OC : All the common knowledge state variables in xt are observable to the econometrician, and all the private information variables in " t are unobservable.

To complete this description of the econometric model we should comment on the sampling framework. In the case of single-agent models, we assumed that the econometrician has a random sample of many agents (e.g., firms, households) behaving according to the model. That is not the case in applications of dynamic strategic games where typically the number of players is quite small, e.g., firms in an oligopoly market, members of a family, political parties, etc. We assume that the game is played independently at different locations, indexed by m , and that we have a random sample of M of these locations. Therefore, taking into account Assumption OC, the data consist of:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Note that an assumption that is implicit in this description of the data is that we observe the actions of all the players in the game. Our next example illustrates models with strategic interactions and is based on Aguirregabiria and Mira (2007).

Example 4 ( An EntryGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Exit Game of Incomplete Information ). The players are firms making decisions on whether to enter, continuing to operate in, or exit from a market. The market is a small local retail market and each active firm operates at one location or store. We observe a random sample of markets, m D 1 ; 2 ; : : : ; M : In

28 See Doraszelski and Satterthwaite (2007) and Aguirregabiria and Mira (2007) for more details.

each market there are N potentially active and infinitely lived firms. Every period all firms decide simultaneously whether to operate their store or not. If firm i in market m operates its store at time t . aimt D 1 /; its variable profits depend on the number of firms that choose to be active and on market demand conditions, as follows: GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RS log . Smt / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RN log GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 C P j 6D i ajmt GLYPH&lt;17&gt; , where GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RS , GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RN are parameters. Market demand conditions are represented by market (population) size, Smt . Market size is common knowledge to firms and observable to the econometrician, and it follows a first order Markov process. The parameters GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RS and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RN measure the sensitivity of variable profits to market size and to the number of active competitors, respectively. 29 Total current profits of an active firm are:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC ; i GLYPH&lt;0&gt; " imt . 1 / is firm i 's fixed operating cost which has two components: GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC ; i is time-invariant and common knowledge, and " imt . 1 / is private information of firm i and time-varying. The term GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;0&gt; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC ; i is an entry cost, where the entry cost parameter GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC ; i is multiplied by GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;0&gt; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; since this cost is paid only by new entrants. If a firm does not operate its store, it can put its capital to other uses. Current profits of a non-active firm, Uimt . 0 / , are equal to the value of the best outside opportunity. We assume that Uimt . 0 / D " imt . 0 / , which is private information of firm i . The choice-specific private information variables, " imt . 0 / and " imt . 1 / , are transitory normally distributed shocks, iid across firms, markets and time with zero mean. 30

The vector of state variables of the game which are observable to the researcher is xmt D . Smt ; amt GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 / where amt GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 D f aim ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 V i D 1 ; 2 ; : : : ; N g are indicators of incumbency status. Incumbency status matters because incumbent firms do not pay entry costs and are thus more likely to operate their store than nonincumbents. The unobservable state variables are the private information shocks " imt : In this game, a firm's strategy function, GLYPH&lt;11&gt; i . xmt ; " imt / , is a binary decision rule. The associated conditional choice probability, P GLYPH&lt;11&gt; i . 1 j xmt / , is the probability that the firm operates in the market.

Aguirregabiria and Mira estimate this model using panel data of Chilean local retail markets. They assume that all potential entrants in each market are identical and consider symmetric equilibria. 31 They estimate the model separately for five different retail industries and analyze how fixed costs, the sensitivity of profits to the number of active firms and the magnitude of sunk costs contribute to explain differences in the number of active firms across industries. As we describe in Section 4, Aguirregabiria and Mira relax Assumption OC by including a time-invariant and market-specific component of market profitability that is common knowledge for firms but unobserved to the researcher. They find that this unobserved market heterogeneity is important. Failure to account for it may lead to implausible estimates of the parameter GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RN , which measures the sensitivity of profits to the number of active competitors, because more profitable markets tend to have a larger number of active firms in equilibrium.

glyph[squaresolid]

29 One may interpret variable profits as the equilibrium payoffs of a one-period static game in which firms with identical products and variable costs compete in quantities (i.e., Cournot).

30 We might write the value of the best outside opportunity as GLYPH&lt;22&gt; i C " imt . 0 /; but the parameter GLYPH&lt;22&gt; i cannot be identified separately from the average fixed cost GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC ; i so we normalize it to zero.

31 Therefore, GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC ; i and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC ; i is the same for all firms. Aguirregabiria et al. (2007a,b) relax this assumption.

In order to illustrate Hotz and Miller's CCP estimation method and its extensions for single-agent problems we will use the following simplified version of Example 4:

Example 4B ( EntryGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Exit Without Strategic Interactions ). In Example 4, suppose that GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RN D 0 such that, conditional on market size St , firms' variable profits are independent of the number of active competitors. This assumption corresponds to markets with either perfect or monopolistic competition. Then, the second term in the payoff function (27) is zero and strategic interactions become irrelevant. Every period the firm has to decide whether to stay active or not taking into account the sunk costs GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC incurred each time it decides to re-enter the market and the stochastic fluctuations in its profits brought about by changes in market size St , by the idiosyncratic shocks to fixed operating costs " it . 1 / , and by the best outside opportunity " it . 0 / . The state vector for firm i is now xit D . St ; ait GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 /; i.e., only the own incumbent status matters. Furthermore, suppose that " it . 1 / and " it . 0 / satisfy assumption CLOGIT rather than being jointly normal. We are also assuming that structural parameters are identical across markets and firms and we have dropped the m subscript so the t subscript now stands for any market-period observation. glyph[squaresolid]

The estimation of dynamic games, relative to single-agent models, poses several specific problems. Simple as Example 4 is, it illustrates how the size of the game's state space tends to grow exponentially with the number of players if it includes playerspecific state variables such as incumbency status. As in the competitive equilibrium model of Example 3, solving the model nests two levels of fixed point problems, i.e., the best response equilibrium condition and the individual player's dynamic programming problem. In order to alleviate the computational complexity, one might attempt to estimate structural parameters from each player's individual decision problem and actions. But the decision rule depends on the (lagged) actions of other players, such as their incumbency status. If some aggregate state variables are unobservable (e.g., the permanent component of market profitability), other player's actions are endogenous. 32 For given parameter values, multiple equilibria are a (likely) possibility which will make the empirical model indeterminate and call for some additional structure.

On a different level, note that Example 4 is different from the first three in that only choice data are used in estimation. In the first three examples, data on payoffs (i.e., wages) was also available. 'Pure choice' data is a fairly common situation which tends to make estimation simpler because payoff data are often choice-censored and corrections for selection can be more difficult to implement in structural models, as we will see for Example 2. On the other hand, it is clear that availability of payoff data is an important source of identification. Furthermore, the basic framework that we have described for empirical discrete games is easily extended to the case in which payoff variables satisfy Assumption CI-Y.

## 3. Estimation methods for single-agent models

## 3.1. Methods for Rust's model

We introduced Rust's model as a single-agent model with the additive separability, iid, conditional independence and logistic assumptions. We describe four methods/algorithms which have been applied to the estimation of this class of models: (1) Rust's nested fixed point algorithm; (2) HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's CCP method;

32 And, furthermore, an initial conditions problem has to be taken into account.

(3) The NPL algorithm, a recursive CCP method Aguirregabiria and Mira, 2002; and (4) A simulation-based CCP method Hotz et al., 1994. The first of these is a full solution method, i.e., the DP problem is solved for every trial value of the parameters. Methods (2)GLYPH&lt;21&gt;(4) avoid repeated full solutions of the DP problem, taking advantage of the existence of an invertible mapping between conditional choice probabilities and differences in choice-specific value functions, a result due to Hotz and Miller (1993). Almost all applications in models with more than two choice alternatives have imposed the CLOGIT assumption on unobservables. Although the CLOGIT assumption per se is not essential for the statistical properties of Rust's model and the methods which we review here, it leads to closed form expressions for several of the econometric model's key objects. This is an advantage which all four of these methodsfully exploit, and CCP methods are specially dependent on it. Extending the range of applicability of NFXP and CCP methods to models which do not impose the CLOGIT assumption is a topic for further research. One step in this direction is the estimation of DPnested logit models and models with generalized extreme value (GEV) unobservables. 33

## 3.1.1. Rust's nested fixed point algorithm

The nested fixed point algorithm (NFXP) is a gradient iterative search method to obtain the maximum likelihood estimator of the structural parameters. More specifically, this algorithm combines a BHHH method (outer algorithm), that searches for a root of the likelihood equations, with a value function or policy iteration method (inner algorithm), that solves the dynamic programming problem for each trial value of the structural parameters. The algorithm is initialized with an arbitrary vector of structural parameters, say O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0. A BHHH iteration is defined as:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where l i .GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ is the log-likelihood function for individual i . Given the form of the likelihood in Eq. (10), and that GLYPH&lt;18&gt; D .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 Y ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f / 0 , we have:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The terms @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y log f Y and @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f log f x are standard because the transition probability function and the payoff function are primitives of the model. However, to obtain @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; log P we need to solve the DP problem for GLYPH&lt;18&gt; D O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; k in order to compute the conditional choice probabilities and their derivatives with respect to the components of O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; k . There are different ways to solve the DP problem. When the model has finite horizon (i.e., T is finite) the standard approach is to use backward induction. For infinite horizon models, one can use either value function iterations (described below) or policy function iterations, or an hybrid of both.

33 See Arcidiacono (2004, 2005) for dynamic structural models with GEV unobservables. Arcidiacono and Miller (2008) explore the use of CCP methods for models with GEV unobservables.

To illustrate this algorithm in more detail, consider a version of Rust's DP conditional logit model with infinite horizon and discrete observable state variables x . Let N V .GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ be the column vector of values f N V . x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ V x 2 X g . Following Eq. (12), this vector of values can be obtained as the unique fixed point in N V of the following Bellman equation in vector form:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where u . a ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ is the column vector of utilities f u . a ; x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ V x 2 X g and F x . a / is the transition probability matrix with elements f x . x 0 j a ; x / for all x and x 0 in X ; where x and x 0 denote the current state and next period's state, respectively. The choice probabilities P . a j x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ have the conditional logit form:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where F x . a ; x / is the column vector f f x . x 0 j a ; x / V x 0 2 X g . To obtain the gradient @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; log Pit of this DP conditional logit model, it is useful to take into account that the denominator in Eq. (31) is equal to the exponential of N V . xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ . It can be shown that this gradient has the following analytic form:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The expression for @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y log Pit is equivalent to that of @ @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u log Pit . And given the Bellman equation in (30), the Jacobian matrix @ N V .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 is:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where P . a j GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ is the column vector of choice probabilities f P . a j x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ V x 2 X g , and GLYPH&lt;3&gt; represents the element-by-element product. Note that we obtain the gradient of the value functions in a relatively simple manner as a by-product of the iterative DP solution method. This is an important additional advantage of the DPconditional logit specification. Without it, it would be necessary to 'perturb' each element of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; and obtain new solutions of the DP model for each perturbation in order to compute numerical derivatives, which is much more costly. 34

The NFXP algorithm proceeds as follows. We start with an arbitrary value of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; , say O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0. Given O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0, in the 'inner' algorithm we

34 If policy iteration is used to solve the infinite horizon DP model, the gradient of the value function can also be obtained as a by-product. The computational cost of computing numerical derivatives is the main reason why BHHH, which avoids second derivatives, is particularly useful in the estimation of structural models.

obtain the vector N V . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 / by successive iterations in the Bellman equation (30): starting with some guess N V 0, we iterate in N V h C 1 D log . P J a D 0 exp f u . a ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 / C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; F x . a / N V h g / until convergence. Then, given O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 and N V . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 / we construct the choice probabilities P . a j x ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 / using the formula in (31), the matrix @ N V . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 using (33), and the gradient @ l i . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; using Eq. (32). Finally, in the 'outer' algorithm we use the gradient @ l i . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; to make a new BHHH iteration to obtain O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 1. Weproceed in this way until the distance between O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; k C 1 and O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; k or the difference in the likelihoods is smaller than a prespecified convergence constant.

When the model has finite horizon, we can solve for the value function, its gradient and choice probabilities using backward induction in the inner algorithm of the NFXP. That is, the sequence of value vectors at ages T , T GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1, etc., can be obtained starting with N V T . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ D log . P J a D 0 exp f u T . a ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ g / , and then using the recursive formula N V t . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ D log . P J a D 0 exp f u t . a ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; F x ; t . a / N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ g / for t GLYPH&lt;20&gt; T GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1. At each iteration the choice probabilities are Pt . a j O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ D exp f ut . a ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; F x ; t . a / 0 N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ g = T P J j D 0 exp f ut . j ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; F x ; t . j / 0 N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ gU , and the gradients have the following recursive forms @ N V t . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u D P J a D 0 P t . a j O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ GLYPH&lt;3&gt; f @ u t . a ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; F x ; t . a / @ N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u g and @ N V t . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f D GLYPH&lt;12&gt; P J a D 0 P t . a j O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ GLYPH&lt;3&gt; f @ F x ; t . a /= @GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; b F x ; t . a /@ N V t C 1 . O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; /=@GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f g .

As any other gradient method, the NFXP algorithm returns a solution to the likelihood equations. In general, the likelihood function of this class of models is not globally concave. Therefore, some global search is necessary to check whether the root of the likelihood equations that has been found is actually the global maximum and not just a local optimum.

We have described the NFXP algorithm in the context of full information MLE (FIML). However, most applications of this algorithm have considered a sequential partial likelihood approach first advocated by Rust. In the first step, the partial likelihoods P i ; t log f Y . yit j ait ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y / and P i ; t log f x . xi ; t C 1 j ait ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f / are separately maximized to obtain estimates of parameters GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f , respectively. This step does not involve solving the DP problem. Given these estimators, in the second step the parameters in GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u are estimated using the NFXP algorithm and the partial likelihood P i ; t log P . ait j xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y ; O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f / . Multiple-step strategies such as this are widely used to alleviate the computational burden of estimation of dynamic programming structural models. The two-step partial likelihood approach for Rust's model relies on Assumptions CI-Y and CI-X as well as on rational expectations. It can greatly simplify the estimation problem in models with many parameters in the transition probabilities. It was used by Rust and Phelan (1997) to estimate the model that we described in Example 1. In that application, the state variables with stochastic transitions were health status, health expenses, marital status and public pension points. The payoff function is the labor earnings equation. There are two main reasons why this sequential approach reduces the cost of estimating these and other models. First, the number of BHHH iterations needed to reach convergence typically increases with the number of parameters that are estimated. Note that BHHH iterations here are particularly costly because they involve the full solution of the dynamic programming problem. And second, the computational cost associated with a global search increases as well with the dimension of the parameter space. Nevertheless, it should be noted that in the sequential partial likelihood approach the calculation of standard errors in the second step correcting for estimation error in the first step is not a trivial task. It is often no more costly to consider a third estimation step consisting of a single FIML-BHHH iteration which, as it is well known, delivers an asymptotically efficient estimator as well as the correct standard errors.

There is a long list of applications which have used the NFXP algorithm to estimate models in Rust's class in different areas. For instance: investment models of machine replacement, as in Rust (1987), Sturm (1991), Das (1992), Kennet (1993, 1994), and Rust and Rothwell (1995); or models of retirement from the labor force, as in Rust and Phelan (1997), and Karlstrom et al. (2004). In health economics, Gilleskie (1998) proposes and estimates a dynamic model of workers' decisions to visit a doctor and to miss work during an episode of acute illness. In economic demography, Ahn (1995) estimates a model of fertility and the value of children; and Kennan and Walker (2005) a model of individual migration decisions.

## 3.1.2. HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's CCP method

The main advantages of the NFXP algorithm are its conceptual simplicity and, more importantly, that it gives the MLE which is asymptotically efficient under the assumptions of the model. The main limitation of this algorithm is its computational cost. In particular, the DP problem has to be solved exactly for each trial value of the structural parameters. Given the cost of solving some DP problems, this characteristic of the algorithm limits the range of applications that are feasible, even in the DP conditional logit case. Hotz and Miller (1993) observed that, under the assumptions of Rust model, it is not necessary to solve the DP problem even once in order to estimate the structural parameters A key idea in their method is that, using nonparametric estimates of choice and transition probabilities, it is possible to obtain a simple representation of the choice-specific value functions v. a ; x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ for values of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; around the true vector of structural parameters (see also Manski, 1993, for a method that exploits a similar idea). This representation is particularly simple and useful for estimation in the DP conditional logit model with linear-in-parameters utility as in Example 4B. We describe HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's method in detail for this case but we also include a brief discussion of alternative specifications including the nonlinear, infinite horizon case. Furthermore, we assume that the dataset does not include payoff variables or, if it does, Assumption CI-Y holds and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; Y has been estimated and subsumed into the known z functions.

Suppose that u . a ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / D z . a ; xit / 0 GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u where z . a ; xit / is a vector of known 'basis' functions. An example of this is the entry/ exit model with no strategic interactions, Example 4B in Section 2. In that model, the decision is binary, the vector of observable state variables is . St ; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 / , and the one-period payoffs are u . 0 ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / D 0 and u . 1 ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / D GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RS log . St / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC GLYPH&lt;0&gt; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC . 1 GLYPH&lt;0&gt; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 / . Therefore, we can write these payoffs as u . a ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / D z . a ; xit / 0 GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u , where z . 0 ; xit / 0 D . 0 ; 0 ; 0 / , z . 1 ; xit / 0 D . log . St / ; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 ; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; . 1 GLYPH&lt;0&gt; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 // , and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u D .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; RS ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; FC ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; EC / 0 . In Hotz and Miller's representation the choice-specific value functions are written as follows: 35

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Q z . a ; xt ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ is the expected and discounted sum of current and future z vectors f z . at C j ; xt C j / V j D 0 ; 1 ; 2 ; : : : g which may occur along all possible histories originating from the choice of a in state xt , if the individual behaves optimally in the future. More formally,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

35 HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's representation can be derived either for alternative-specific value functions or for the integrated value function (Emax function). We have preferred to use the first version here.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where GLYPH&lt;11&gt; . GLYPH&lt;1&gt; / is the optimal decision rule as defined in Section 2 and Ext C j j at D a ; xt . GLYPH&lt;1&gt; / represents the expectation over the distribution of xt C j conditional on . at D a ; xt /; given that state variables evolve as a controlled stochastic process defined by the optimal decision rule and transition probability functions. Likewise, Q e . a ; xt ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ is the expected and discounted sum of the stream f ". at C j / V j D 1 ; 2 ; : : : g along the histories originating at . at D a ; xt / if the individual behaves optimally in the future.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The function e . a ; xt / is the expectation of " t . a / conditional on xt and on alternative a being optimal, i.e., e . a ; xt / GLYPH&lt;17&gt; E ." t . a / j xt ; GLYPH&lt;11&gt; . xt ; " t / D a / . Hotz and Miller showed that the conditional expectation e . a ; xt / is a function of choice probabilities and the distribution G " only. To see why, first note that the event f GLYPH&lt;11&gt; . xt ; " t / D a g is equivalent to f v. a ; xt / C " t . a / GLYPH&lt;21&gt; v. a 0 ; xt / C " t . a 0 / for any a 0 6D a g . Therefore,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This expression shows that e . a ; xt / depends on the choice probability P . a j xt / , the distribution of " t , and the vector of value differences Q v. xt / GLYPH&lt;17&gt; f v. a ; xt / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; v. 0 ; xt / V a 2 A g . Second, choice probabilities depend on the primitives of the model only through the probability distribution G " and the vector of value differences Q v. xt / : i.e., P . a j xt / D R I f " t . a 0 / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; " t . a / GLYPH&lt;20&gt; v. a ; xt / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; v. a 0 ; xt / 8 a 0 6D a g d G "." t / . Hotz and Miller proved that this mapping relating choice probabilities and value differences is invertible (see Proposition 1, page 501, in Hotz and Miller (1993)). Thus, solving this inverse mapping into Eq. (37), we have that e . a ; xt / is a function of choice probabilities and the distribution G " only. The particular form of the mapping that relates CCPs and value differences depends on the probability distribution G " . For instance, in the model of Example 4B, v. 1 ; xt / (or v. 0 ; xt / ) is the firm's expected stream of profits if it decides to be active (or not active) in state xt and behave optimally thereafter. For this binary choice model, Q v. xt / D v. 1 ; xt / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; v. 0 ; xt / . Under assumption CLOGIT on G " the probability that the firm will be active is P . 1 j xt / D exp . Q v. xt // 1 C exp . Q v. xt // . Inverting this relationship we get Q v. xt / D log . P . 1 j xt // GLYPH&lt;0&gt; log . P . 0 j xt // . Also, under the CLOGIT assumption, the conditional expectation e . 1 ; xt / is equal to GLYPH&lt;13&gt; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; log GLYPH&lt;16&gt; exp . Q v. xt // 1 C exp . Q v. xt // GLYPH&lt;17&gt; , where GLYPH&lt;13&gt; is Euler's constant, and similarly e . 0 ; xt / D GLYPH&lt;13&gt; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; log GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 1 C exp . Q v. xt // GLYPH&lt;17&gt; : Using the inverse mapping above, it turns out that e . a ; xt / D GLYPH&lt;13&gt; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; log P . a j xt ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ .

Note that Q z . a ; xt ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ and Q e . a ; xt ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ depend on GLYPH&lt;18&gt; only through the parameters in the transition probabilities, GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f , and the vector of CCPs, P .GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ D f P . a j x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ V . a ; x / 2 A GLYPH&lt;2&gt; X g . In fact, we can define Q z and Q e for an arbitrary vector of CCPs (optimal or not).

To emphasize this point we use the notation Q z P . a ; xt / to represent P T GLYPH&lt;0&gt; t j D 1 GLYPH&lt;12&gt; j Ext C j j at D a ; xt T P J a 0 D 0 P . a 0 j xt C j / z . a 0 ; xt C j / U and Q e P . a ; xt / to represent P T GLYPH&lt;0&gt; t j D 1 GLYPH&lt;12&gt; j Ext C j j at D a ; xt T P J a 0 D 0 P . a 0 j xt C j / e . a 0 ; xt C j / U . 36

Let GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 D .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f / be the true value of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; in the population of individuals under study. And let P 0 be the conditional choice probabilities in the population. If we knew . P 0 ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f / , we could construct the values Q z P 0 . a ; x / and Q e P 0 . a ; x / and then use Hotz and Miller's representation to approximate the choice-specific values v. a ; x ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ as simple linear functions of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u close to its true value. Although we do not know . P 0 ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f / , we can estimate them consistently without having to solve the DP problem. Consistent estimates of transition probabilities can be obtained using a (partial) MLE of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f that maximizes the (partial) likelihood P i ; t log f x . xi ; t C 1 j ait ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f / . Conditional choice probabilities can be estimated using nonparametric regression methods (i.e., P 0 . a j x / D E . I f ait D a gj xit D x / ) such as a NadarayaGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Watson kernel estimator or a simple frequency estimator. Let O P and O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f be the estimators of P 0 and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 f , respectively. Based on these estimates, Hotz and Miller's idea is to approximate v. a ; xit ; GLYPH&lt;18&gt;/ by Q z O P . a ; xit /GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u CQ e O P . a ; xit / in order to obtain the CCP's in Eq. (31). They propose the GMM estimator that solves in GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u the sample moment conditions:

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where H . xit / is a matrix with dimension dim .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / GLYPH&lt;2&gt; J with functions of xit which are used as instruments. For instance, in the entryGLYPH&lt;21&gt;exit model of Example 4B the vector GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u has three parameters, the decision is binary . J D 1 / , and the vector of observable state variables is . St ; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 / . Therefore, a possible choice for H . xit / is the vector . 1 ; St ; ai ; t GLYPH&lt;0&gt; 1 / 0 .

The main advantage of this estimator is its computational simplicity. Nonparametric estimation of choice probabilities is a (relatively) simple task. The main task is the computation of the values Q z O P . a ; xit / and Q e O P . a ; xit /; based on a recursive version of expressions (35) and (36) GLYPH&lt;22&gt; more details are provided below. However, these values are calculated just once and remain fixed in the search for the HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller estimator. In contrast, in a full solution method such as the NFXP algorithm these values are recomputed exactly for each trial value of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; . Thus, HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's methodgreatlyreducesthecomputationalburdenofNFXP's'inner' algorithm. Another important advantage of HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's CCP estimator is that, for the DP conditional logit model with linearin-parameters utility, the system of equations (38) that defines the estimator has a unique solution. Therefore, a global search is not needed.

Previous conventional wisdom was that HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's estimator achieved a significant computational gain at the expense of

36 For notational simplicity we omit GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f as an argument, though it should be clear that Q z P . a ; xt / and Q e P . a ; xt / depend on P and GLYPH&lt;18&gt; f .

efficiency, both in finite samples and asymptotically. Thus, researchers had the choice between two extremes: a full solution NFXP-ML estimator with the attendant computational burden, or the much faster but less efficient HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller estimator. However, Aguirregabiria and Mira (2002) showed that a pseudo-maximum likelihood version of HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's estimator is asymptotically equivalent to partial MLE. The 'two-step' pseudo-maximum likelihood (PML) estimator is defined as the value of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u that maximizes the pseudo-likelihood function 37 :

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The asymptotic variance of this two-step PML estimator is just equal to the variance of the partial MLE of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u described at the end of Section 3.3.1. That is, the initial nonparametric estimator of P 0 and the PML estimator of GLYPH&lt;18&gt; 0 u are asymptotically independent and therefore there is no asymptotic efficiency loss from using an inefficient initial estimator of P 0 . Nevertheless, although the twostep PML estimator is asymptotically equivalent to partial MLE, Monte Carlo experiments show that its finite sample bias can be much larger. 38 Imprecise initial estimates of choice probabilities do not affect the asymptotic properties of the estimator, but they can generate serious small sample biases in the two-step PML estimator and, more generally, in the whole class of HotzGLYPH&lt;21&gt;Miller's CCP estimators. 39 The source of this bias is well understood in two-step methods: O P enters nonlinearly in the sample moment conditions that define the estimator, and the expected value of a nonlinear function of O P is not equal to that function evaluated at the expected value of O P . The larger the variance of O P , the larger the bias of O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u . The variance of the nonparametric estimator of P 0 . a j x / D E . I f ait D a gj xit D x / increases with the number of cells in the set X . In applications with millions of cells in X and a few thousand observations, the variance of O P and the bias of O GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u can be very large. A recursive extension of the two-step method, which we describe in the next subsection, deals with this problem.

We now describe in some detail on the computation of the values Q z P . a ; xt / and Q e P . a ; xt / . As defined in Eqs. (35) and (36), these

For a finite horizon model we can obtain the sequence of values fQ z P t . a ; xt / and Q e P t . a ; xt / V t D 1 ; 2 ; : : : ; T g using backwards induction. Starting at the last period, we have Q z O P T . a ; xT / D z . a ; xT / and Q e O P T . a ; xT / D 0. For every t &lt; T we iterate on Eq. (40). The computational burden incurred is equivalent to that of solving the finite horizon DP problem once.

For stationary infinite horizon models such as Example 4B, we can rewrite Eq. (40) as follows: Q z P . a ; x / D z . a ; x / C GLYPH&lt;12&gt; P x 0 2 X f x . x 0 j a ; x / W P z . x 0 / I and Q e P . a ; x / D GLYPH&lt;12&gt; P x 0 2 X f x . x 0 j a ; x / W P e . x 0 / , where in turn W P z . x / D P J a 0 D 0 P . a j x / Q z P . a ; x / is a 1 GLYPH&lt;2&gt; dim .GLYPH&lt;18&gt; u / vector; and W P e . x / D P J a 0 D 0 P . a j x / GLYPH&lt;0&gt; e . a ; x / C Q e P . a 0 ; x / GLYPH&lt;1&gt; is a scalar. Both W P z . x / and W P e . x / are basis functions for a policy valuation operator. 40 Define the matrix W P GLYPH&lt;17&gt; fT W P z . x /; W P e . x / U V x 2 X g . Then, one can show that W P is the unique solution to the following equation:
